title: 4 — The Shang: China's First Historical Dynasty

sections.title: When the Shang first become historical

paragraphs.text:
The Shang was the first Chinese dynasty to leave written records of its own.
But those records survive only for the final nine kings, so firm history begins only in the late Shang, above all from the reign of Wu Ding to the fall of Di Xin.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The full dynastic frame within which the late Shang archive survives as only the final, best-lit portion.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The phase in which the royal inscriptions, cemeteries, workshops, and temples at Anyang bring the Shang court clearly into view.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order that conquered Shang yet inherited many of its ritual, political, and scribal habits.

timeline.label: Wu Ding
timeline.caption: The ruler under whom the late Shang archive becomes especially rich and historically revealing.

timeline.label: Di Xin
timeline.caption: The last Shang ruler, whose fall marks the end of the dynasty as an independent kingdom.

paragraphs.text:
Much in Shang culture had deeper roots in Henan Longshan and in other late Neolithic worlds to the east and northeast.
Even so, it is only with the late Shang inscriptions that one can speak with confidence of a recognizably early Chinese civilization, with strong ritual hierarchy, political concentration, and mature art.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Henan Longshan
timeline.caption: A late Neolithic horizon from which important cultural and political elements later flowed into the Shang world.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The earlier dynastic horizon before the rich Anyang inscriptions, still only partly visible through archaeology and later reconstruction.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The phase in which the royal inscriptions, cemeteries, workshops, and temples at Anyang bring the Shang court clearly into view.

paragraphs.text:
Later tradition claimed that the dynasty was called Shang before Pan Geng moved the capital, and Yin afterward.
The contemporary evidence does not support that.
The late inscriptions speak of Shang, not of a place called Yin, so Yin seems to have been, at least in part, a Zhou name for the dynasty they replaced.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The earlier dynastic horizon before the rich Anyang inscriptions, still only partly visible through archaeology and later reconstruction.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The phase in which the royal inscriptions, cemeteries, workshops, and temples at Anyang bring the Shang court clearly into view.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order that conquered Shang yet inherited many of its ritual, political, and scribal habits.

timeline.label: Pan Geng
timeline.display_date: traditionally placed in the thirteenth century BC
timeline.caption: Later tradition connected him with the move to the site later remembered as Yin, though the evidence remains partly retrospective.

paragraphs.text:
The dynastic name itself remains obscure.
It may have come from a place, as later dynastic names often did.
Later tradition linked the founding ancestor Xie to a place called Shang, while some scholars have connected the name to a river power mentioned in the bones.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Predynastic Shang lineage
timeline.caption: Later tradition projected a remembered ancestral background behind the ruling house.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The historical dynasty later writers tried to explain by tracing its name and origin back before its own records.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang inscriptions are the first firm internal evidence against which such traditional explanations can be judged.

paragraphs.text:
Eastern Zhou writers knew surprisingly little in detail about Shang life.
Confucius was said to regret that the surviving records of Song, the Shang successor state, were too thin to restore Shang ritual in full.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The full dynastic frame within which the late Shang archive survives as only the final, best-lit portion.

timeline.label: Eastern Zhou
timeline.caption: The later Zhou age in which classical thinkers and transmitters looked back on Shang from a great distance.

timeline.label: Confucius
timeline.caption: Later tradition made him a witness to how partial and fragile detailed Shang ritual memory had become.

paragraphs.text:
Sima Qian's Basic Annals of Yin gave the dynasty a continuous story, from the miraculous conception of Xie to the fall of the last ruler at Muye and the failed rebellion of Wu Geng.
The narrative is rich in some places and skeletal in others, and it preserves moral coloring as well as memory.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The full dynastic frame within which the late Shang archive survives as only the final, best-lit portion.

timeline.label: Han dynasty
timeline.caption: The age in which early imperial historians organized Shang memory into a continuous dynastic narrative.

timeline.label: Sima Qian
timeline.caption: His Basic Annals of Yin gave later readers the first full continuous narrative of the dynasty.

timeline.label: Battle of Muye
timeline.caption: The Zhou victory at Muye ended the Shang kingdom but not the wider life of Shang institutions.

paragraphs.text:
Later texts did preserve something important.
They generally remembered the temple names of the Shang kings and the order in which the kings stood.
Figure 4.1 helps one see that remembered lineage as a structured chain rather than a blur of names.

figures.label: Figure 4.1
figures.caption: The genealogy turns remembered temple names into a visible structure, making the royal line easier to follow than a mere list of names.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The remembered king list belongs to the full dynastic frame, not only to its best-documented final phase.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang sacrifices make this lineage more concrete because the court itself names and orders many of the dead kings.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: After the conquest, Zhou transmitters preserved and reshaped this remembered royal chain.

timeline.label: Wu Ding
timeline.caption: A central late Shang node in the lineage, richly illuminated by contemporary records.

timeline.label: Di Xin
timeline.caption: The last king closes the line at the moment of dynastic collapse.

paragraphs.text:
But the transmitted account is not enough by itself.
It did not know many of the persons, events, and anxieties that appear plainly in the oracle bones.
Here the listener begins to feel the difference between later reconstruction and a dynasty speaking in its own records.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The oracle bones preserve the dynasty's own voice from its final, best-lit phase.

timeline.label: Han retrospective reconstruction
timeline.caption: A much later narrative horizon that reconstructed the Shang past through transmitted texts and moralized dynastic memory.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor Zhou order stands between the Shang world itself and the much later texts that tried to reconstruct it.

paragraphs.text:
The bronzes remain invaluable for the late Shang world.
Their distribution, style, and provenance reveal the range of Shang culture and its regional variation.
Yet the number of long bronze inscriptions that truly narrate Shang history is small.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the Anyang archive, archaeology and bronzes carry much more of the historical load.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Within the late Shang, bronzes reveal both the reach of Shang culture and its regional variation.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Many bronze-working habits and forms continued into the successor age, though the historical setting changed.

paragraphs.text:
The oracle bones are different.
Here the Shang court speaks much more directly.
Diviners heated prepared cattle scapulas and turtle shells until the surface cracked, then read those cracks as signs of approval or danger from the Powers.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the rich Anyang archive, no other Shang evidence lets the court speak so directly.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, divination on bone and shell became one of the central institutions of rule.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later states inherited divination and writing, but not this exact royal pyromantic system.

paragraphs.text:
These inscriptions were lost to historical memory for many centuries and were only recognized for what they were in the late Qing.
More than two hundred thousand fragments were eventually recovered from Xiaotun near Anyang.
Together with palaces, workshops, and elite burials, they reveal this zone as the great sacro-administrative center of the late Shang.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The archive itself belongs to the late Shang court at Anyang.

timeline.label: Modern rediscovery of oracle bones
timeline.caption: The modern era in which lost oracle-bone inscriptions were recognized, collected, and excavated again.

timeline.label: Recognition of oracle bones
timeline.caption: The modern recognition of oracle bones restored the Shang court's own written archive to history.

paragraphs.text:
If later tradition is right, Xiaotun formed part of the center later called Yinxu and served as the capital for the last twelve kings, beginning with Pan Geng.
But archaeology has not securely confirmed a pre-Wu Ding dynastic Shang presence there.
Even here, then, the evidence has edges.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The earlier dynastic horizon before the rich Anyang inscriptions, still only partly visible through archaeology and later reconstruction.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The final Anyang phase is securely attested; earlier dynastic occupation at the same site remains less certain.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: After the conquest, later memory fixed the site under the retrospective name Yin.

timeline.label: Pan Geng
timeline.display_date: traditionally placed in the thirteenth century BC
timeline.caption: Later tradition connected him with the move to the site later remembered as Yin, though the evidence remains partly retrospective.

paragraphs.text:
Royal divination also rested on a large material apparatus.
Scapulas and shells had to be acquired, sometimes in tribute, animals and turtles had to be killed, surfaces cleaned, hollows drilled, rites performed, records carved, and used bones finally buried.
All this shows that divination was not a marginal curiosity.
It was one of the central institutions of Shang rule.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier Shang polities left no surviving institution documented at this material scale.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang court made divination a large, labor-intensive, and highly organized enterprise.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The conquest ended the dynasty, but not the wider Chinese linkage between writing, ritual, and rule.

paragraphs.text:
After the cracks were made and interpreted, the result was carefully recorded.
The crack numbers might be carved beside the marks, specialists then incised the divination text, and the grooves and characters could even be filled with pigment.
Figure 4.3 shows this system clearly, while Figure 4.4 preserves a fuller divination record from the reign of Wu Ding.

figures.label: Figure 4.3
figures.caption: The shell shows how charge, crack, and written record were materially tied together in one divinatory object.

figures.label: Figure 4.4
figures.caption: This fuller record from Wu Ding's reign shows how prediction, forecast, and outcome could all be preserved together.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the mature Anyang archive, no such complete written divination records survive.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The careful recording of cracks and texts belongs to the late Shang court's fully developed mantic system.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: The richest surviving examples come especially from the reign of Wu Ding.

paragraphs.text:
The surviving inscriptions also let scholars identify diviners as historical actors.
More than one hundred twenty diviners are known for the last nine Shang kings.
Some seem to have belonged to named lineages or communities, which suggests that this work carried standing rather than menial obscurity.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The wider history of Shang ritual is mostly silent about individual diviners before the late Anyang archive.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang inscriptions, diviners emerge as visible historical actors with names and affiliations.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order inherited writing and ritual offices, but not this exact late Shang divinatory profile.

paragraphs.text:
Modern scholars divide these inscriptions into major diviner groups and broader chronological periods by style, topic, and formula.
By the end of the dynasty, however, the king himself had become almost the sole diviner named in the record.
What had once been shared more widely now gathered into the monarch's own hands.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: Under Wu Ding, divinatory activity still involved a broader field of named specialists.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: By the final kings, the monarch's own person increasingly monopolized the role of diviner.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou conquest ended this particular royal concentration of mantic authority.

sections.title: From open inquiry to ritual routine

paragraphs.text:
In the earlier late Shang, especially under Wu Ding, divination often proceeded through paired charges.
One stated a hope or prediction in positive form, and the other stated its negative counterpart.
Figure 4.2 and Figure 4.3 show this bilateral logic in a harvest divination, and Figure 4.5 shows the same form used for a royal childbirth.

figures.label: Figure 4.2
figures.caption: The shell preserves the king's favorable forecast on the reverse side of a harvest divination.

figures.label: Figure 4.3
figures.caption: The front side shows the paired positive and negative charges laid out across the same divinatory surface.

figures.label: Figure 4.5
figures.caption: A royal childbirth divination shows that the same paired logic governed intimate dynastic concerns as well as harvest.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The paired form belongs to the late Shang divinatory world as a whole.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: Under Wu Ding, the bilateral style appears especially fully and across a wide range of subjects.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: By the final kings, much of this exploratory breadth had narrowed into more regularized routine.

paragraphs.text:
These paired charges ranged across a remarkably wide field.
They touched harvest, war, weather, dreams, sickness, childbirth, military plans, travel, settlement building, tribute, and the intentions of Di and other powers.
The symmetry of Shang divination fits well with the wider Shang taste for balanced opposition in art and thought.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier Shang phases are much less visible, so the late Shang archive carries most of the evidence for this mode of thought.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang court framed matters through balanced opposition in divination no less than in art.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later political orders inherited Shang forms selectively, but not this same dense bilateral mantic habit.

paragraphs.text:
Later in the dynasty, much of this breadth contracted.
Period Five divinations focused far more on scheduled ancestral rites, on wishes for no harm in the coming ten-day week, and on royal hunts performed on fixed days.
Many earlier concerns vanished from the record.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: The reign of Wu Ding preserves the widest surviving range of divinatory concerns.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: By the closing phase of the dynasty, many earlier concerns disappear from the record.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou conquest ended the dynasty before this narrower late pattern could develop further.

paragraphs.text:
The form changed too.
The script became smaller.
The number of cracks per charge was reduced.
Crack notations largely disappeared.
Royal forecasts grew shorter and more uniformly auspicious.
What had once been exploratory and wide-ranging became more regulated, more selective, and more optimistic in tone.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: The earlier late Shang still preserves fuller crack notation, larger script, and more exploratory forecasts.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: The later late Shang compresses notation and moves toward shorter, more uniformly favorable statements.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: This increasingly routinized mantic style disappears with the fall of the dynasty.

paragraphs.text:
These changes probably belonged to larger transformations within Shang life.
Ancestral cult became more systematized, succession became more regular, kingship grew more concentrated, and divination itself moved toward narrower ritual routine.
That is why the bones tell us more about the fears and decisions of Wu Ding than about those of the final kings.
As the practice became more orderly, it also became less revealing.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang archive as a whole lets one see divination growing more selective over time.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: Wu Ding's reign remains the richest window onto Shang fears, questions, and decisions.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: The final kings leave a thinner and more regularized record of royal concern.

paragraphs.text:
The bones were not powerful simply because they were turtle shell or ox bone.
The stronger impression from the evidence is that they became effective because they were consecrated and used in rites carried out before the ancestors.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier sacred practices remain only dimly visible before the mature Anyang archive.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, consecrated bones became effective within a carefully ancestral ritual setting.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later ancestor cults retained the broader idea of ritual mediation, though in different institutional forms.

paragraphs.text:
One may suppose that the ancestor in whose temple the rite was performed acted as an intermediary.
The petition rose upward through that ancestral presence toward other powers above.
Even the material shape of the turtle shell may have carried symbolic force, though that particular idea rests more on later analogy than on firm Shang proof.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier cosmological ideas must mostly be inferred before the late Shang archive speaks clearly.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, petitions appear to move upward through a hierarchized field of ancestral mediation.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou inherited and reshaped this ancestral ordering rather than abolishing it.

paragraphs.text:
The charges themselves suggest that the dead were arranged in a hierarchy.
Junior ancestors might be asked to protect the king's person or his hunts.
Senior dynastic ancestors were invoked when harvest, invasion, or victory in war was at stake.
Figure 4.6 shows the late form of this practice in a highly regularized weekly divination.

figures.label: Figure 4.6
figures.caption: The scapula shows the terse, regular, and highly systematized weekly divination of the dynasty's closing phase.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The hierarchy among ancestors is clearest in the late Shang inscriptions.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: Figure 4.6 belongs to the final phase, when weekly divination had become especially regularized.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order inherited ancestral hierarchy, but not this exact late Shang divinatory form.

paragraphs.text:
The diviners framed the charges and oversaw the rite, but the king usually alone read the cracks.
In the bones he could even speak of himself as "I, the one man."
The monopoly mattered.
The final act of interpretation belonged to the ruler whose kinship tie to the ancestors gave him unique authority.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier rulers left no surviving archive that makes their interpretive authority equally visible.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, the king's unique reading of the cracks made ritual kinship into political authority.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later dynasties preserved royal mediation with the dead, but under different ideological forms.

paragraphs.text:
Absolute Shang chronology still rests on a combination of different kinds of evidence.
Scholars bring together oracle bones, later bronze inscriptions, transmitted Zhou accounts, and the broader limits supplied by radiocarbon dating.
No single line of evidence settles the matter by itself.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: For the earlier Shang, chronology depends more heavily on reconstruction from archaeology and later texts.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang provides the firmest anchors because inscriptions, bronzes, and archaeology begin to converge.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The conquest supplies the clearest outer boundary for the end of the dynasty.

paragraphs.text:
The eclipse inscriptions suggest that Wu Ding was on the throne at some point between at least 1198 and 1189 BC.
That gives a firm anchor for the late Shang, but not a complete regnal table.
For that reason many historians prefer to work outward from Wu Ding, the last king Di Xin, and a Zhou conquest placed around 1045 BC.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Late Shang chronology becomes firmer where eclipse evidence, named kings, and inscriptional sequence meet.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou overthrow gives the last strong fixed point from which late Shang dates are often reckoned.

timeline.label: Wu Ding
timeline.caption: The ruler under whom the late Shang archive becomes especially rich and historically revealing.

timeline.label: Zhou conquest of Shang
timeline.caption: The overthrow of Shang provides the clearest outer anchor for late Shang chronology.

paragraphs.text:
The dynasty's beginning is still less secure.
Astronomical arguments have been used to place Cheng Tang's first year in 1554 BC, while other estimates keep the beginning nearer 1600.
The end of the dynasty has also long been disputed, with older calculations ranging widely before more recent arguments converged near 1045 or 1046 BC.
The general historical frame is clear, but exact dating is still partly a matter of reconstruction.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The full dynastic span is clear in broad outline even where exact regnal years remain debated.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The last part of the dynasty is much more securely anchored than its beginning.

timeline.label: Proposed date for Cheng Tang's first year
timeline.caption: Astronomical reconstruction has sometimes placed his first year here, but the dynasty's beginning remains disputed.

timeline.label: Zhou conquest of Shang
timeline.caption: The end date, though once widely debated, now converges much more closely around the mid-eleventh century BC.

paragraphs.text:
In practice, scholars often know the order of inscriptions more securely than their exact years.
Temple names, archaeological context, hollow shapes, diviner names, writing style, same-bone links, and changes in formula all help place one inscription before or after another.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the mature archive, relative sequence is often even harder to recover than exact year dating.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, names, styles, formulas, and archaeological context make relative ordering more secure.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The conquest ends the sequence, giving later scholars a clear terminal horizon for relative dating.

paragraphs.text:
That is why oracle bones are commonly grouped not only by kings, but also by diviner groups.
Changes in style did not always coincide neatly with a change of reign.
Even so, the older five-period scheme remains useful for broad historical orientation.
It provides a workable framework for following the growth and narrowing of late Shang divination over time.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The five-period scheme is a way of organizing the late Shang archive rather than a direct replacement for exact dates.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: The earliest late Shang phase still preserves a broader and more various divinatory world.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: The final phase shows how far divination had narrowed and regularized by the end of the dynasty.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou conquest closes the field within which the five-period scheme operates.

paragraphs.text:
For the Shang, time was never merely practical.
It governed hunts, births, labor drafts, and agricultural work, but it also ordered sacrifice, omen, luck, and the proper moment for approaching the powers.
Human time and sacred time were woven together.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the richly documented late court, early Shang timekeeping remains much harder to see directly.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, practical and sacred time were tightly interwoven in the surviving record.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later Chinese states inherited this fusion of calendar, ritual timing, and political order in transformed form.

paragraphs.text:
Even the division of the day seems to have remained descriptive rather than mechanical.
Some terms referred to the movement of light, such as dawn or midday.
Others referred to social moments, such as the small meal.
The effect is not that of a clockwork schedule, but of a lived day marked by ritual and activity.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The earlier evolution of daily time terms is only dimly recoverable before the late archive.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Late Shang vocabulary still marks the day through light, meals, and activity rather than through mechanical timekeeping.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later calendar systems would become more explicit and computational than this lived late Shang day.

paragraphs.text:
The larger cycle was more formal.
Days were named by combining the ten stems with the twelve branches, producing a repeating cycle of sixty days.
Those day names were not bare labels.
They entered the ancestral cult itself, because dead kings and queens received temple names built from the same stem system and were increasingly worshipped on their own name days.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The stem-branch system became one of the dynasty's most durable temporal inheritances.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, day naming entered directly into the ancestral cult through temple names and ritual timing.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The sixty-day cycle long outlived the dynasty and remained central to later Chinese calendrical practice.

paragraphs.text:
At the center of this order stood the ten-day week, the xun.
It began on a jia day and ended on a gui day, and the recurring wish that "in the next ten days there will be no disasters" seems to have been divined throughout the whole historical late Shang.
By the end of the dynasty, the growing cycle of ancestral offerings also served as a rough ritual year of about 360 or 370 days.
Intercalary months were used to keep lunar months and the solar year in step, but the system was still developing.
It seems to have been moving from close observation toward more explicit calculation, without yet reaching the mathematical regularity of later calendars.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The ten-day week and stem-branch cycle are visible as dynastic institutions, not merely as late curiosities.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: By the late Shang, the xun and the growing ancestral schedule together created a rough ritual year.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later calendars would regularize and calculate more fully what still appears partly observational in the Shang world.

paragraphs.text:
From the reign of Wu Ding onward, Xiaotun was far more than a settlement.
It was the dynasty's great sacro-administrative center.
Here stood royal burial grounds, temple foundations, bronze workshops, diviners, engravers, record keepers, and the allied communities that clustered around the court.
Figure 4.7 shows the scale and ordered concentration of that center.

figures.label: Figure 4.7
figures.caption: The plan gathers temple foundations, ditches, river setting, and clustered activity into one view of the late Shang center at Xiaotun.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before Anyang, no other Shang center is documented with this same combination of inscriptions, workshops, burials, and palatial remains.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Xiaotun was the great sacro-administrative center of the late Shang court.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited the idea of a ritual-political center even after this particular Shang node was lost.

timeline.label: Xiaotun cult center
timeline.caption: The temple, archive, workshop, and burial center near Anyang where late Shang rule was concentrated and remembered.

paragraphs.text:
The site mattered not only because power was exercised there, but because knowledge was made there.
Oracle bones used on journeys and hunts were brought back to Xiaotun for storage and burial.
That alone suggests that the court regarded this place as the privileged point where political action and ritual memory met.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier Shang centers do not leave a comparable archive of ritual memory and political action in one place.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: At Xiaotun, political action and written ritual memory were physically gathered into the same center.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later states would continue to bind archive, court, and ritual memory together, though in different forms.

paragraphs.text:
At the top of the Shang pantheon stood Di, the high power whose original character remains difficult to define.
Di could send down approval or disaster.
Above all, Di could command rain, thunder, and wind in a way no lesser power could.
The resemblance to the political world is striking.
Just as only the king issued orders among men, so only Di fully issued orders among the powers.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the late Shang archive, the place of Di in the upper pantheon is far harder to define.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang record, Di stands at the top of the divine field as the highest power of command.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later Zhou religious language transformed but did not erase this problem of supreme heavenly authority.

paragraphs.text:
Below Di stood a wider and less sharply bounded cluster of powers.
These included natural powers such as the River, the Mountain, the Earth, and the Sun, together with Former Lords, predynastic ancestors, dynastic kings, and dynastic queens.
The boundaries between these categories were not rigid.
Some higher powers were treated in quasi-ancestral ways, yet none of them simply collapsed into the others.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier religious orders must mostly be inferred before the late court speaks explicitly.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang upper pantheon is a loose but ordered field rather than a simple, tidy ladder.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later political theology would inherit some of these distinctions while reorganizing them under different terms.

paragraphs.text:
The Former Lords are especially revealing.
Figures such as Wang Hai and Yi Yin stood outside the regular ancestral cycle, yet received cult and retained great prestige.
The predynastic ancestors formed another distinct layer, marked by unusually regular temple names and by a cult pattern that differed from that of the later kings.
The upper pantheon was therefore not a simple ladder.
It was a loose but ordered field of powers whose jurisdictions overlapped and whose favor had to be negotiated case by case.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Predynastic and early dynastic ancestors belong to a deeper layer behind the best-documented late cult.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang record preserves Former Lords, predynastic ancestors, and dynastic kings within one overlapping field of powers.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: After the conquest, Zhou memory and ritual practice would continue to filter and reorder this inherited sacred hierarchy.

paragraphs.text:
The ancestors were the living skeleton of the Shang polity.
To name them in divination and to feed them in sacrifice was not merely to remember the dead.
It was to reaffirm the lineage through which the king ruled and from which he drew suprahuman backing.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The whole dynasty can be read as a lineage order sustained through its dead.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang inscriptions, this ancestral skeleton becomes especially visible and politically explicit.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited ancestor-centered legitimacy on a scale the Shang had already powerfully formed.

paragraphs.text:
The main line of descent mattered most.
Kings whose fathers and sons were both kings formed the central line later called the great lineage.
Those kings and their consorts received the strongest and most durable ritual support.
Collateral rulers could be downgraded, and shifts in how a dead king was treated could mirror shifts in political value among the living.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: Main and collateral lines belonged to the full dynastic structure, not only to its last generations.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang cult makes the hierarchy of descent unusually clear.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later Chinese rulership continued to depend on differentiating direct and collateral lines in both ritual and politics.

paragraphs.text:
These distinctions reached even into titles.
Only kings on the direct line could receive the title Di attached to their temple names.
Only the consorts of main-line kings were securely maintained in the great cycle of rites.
Such arrangements made a political principle visible in sacred form.
Father to son succession was strengthened not only by force and memory, but by the way the dead themselves were ordered.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: Temple naming and the grading of ancestors belong to the dynasty's wider structure of remembered power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, direct descent and the title Di become tightly bound within the cult.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order kept ancestral hierarchy central even while reorganizing its ideological language.

paragraphs.text:
The ancestors were represented by spirit tablets or their Shang precursors and housed in temples.
There were temples for major kings, for some queens, for certain higher powers, and also larger joint temples in which groups of ancestors received cult together.
Main-line temples were maintained across generations.
Collateral temples seem to have been far less enduring.
The built cultic landscape itself expressed the hierarchy of the lineage.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the mature archive, temple arrangements are much less securely visible.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang built cultic space itself into an expression of lineage hierarchy.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later dynasties would continue to organize royal memory through differentiated temples and ritual placement.

sections.title: Sacrifice and the social life of lineage

paragraphs.text:
The cult was materially lavish and socially thick.
Millet ale, grain, cattle, dogs, sheep, and at times human victims were offered in rites that sought rain, harvest, childbirth, military success, protection, or release from danger.
Bronze vessels for heating and pouring drink make clear that wine stood near the center of this ritual world.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The sacrificial world behind the late court had deeper roots, though it is far less fully documented before Anyang.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, lavish offerings of grain, ale, animals, and sometimes humans stood near the center of political religion.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order inherited the fusion of ritual offering and state life, even as forms and emphases changed.

paragraphs.text:
These offerings were not made in isolation.
Some rites presented the king as host to his ancestors, while others had the form of a feast or banquet.
The same word could be used when the king entertained living supporters.
One begins to see that sacrifice also bound the living together around the dead.
To share in the cult of the royal descent group was to stand close to the source of dynastic power itself.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: Across the dynasty, sacrifice bound the living to the dead and organized status around that bond.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang court, offerings also worked as feasts that gathered the living around royal ancestors.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou inherited and extended this linkage between lineage solidarity and sacrificial order.

paragraphs.text:
Cult to the Shang ancestors revolved around a recurring sequence of five rites.
Because these rites were regularly divined, especially in Periods Two and Five, they help scholars reconstruct both the Shang calendar and the order of the kings.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The five-ritual cycle belongs to the late Shang cultic world as a whole.

timeline.label: Period Two divination
timeline.caption: An important phase in which the five-ritual cycle is frequently recorded and helps reconstruct Shang ritual order.

timeline.label: Period Five divination
timeline.caption: The final late Shang phase, marked by more systematized ritual recording and narrower divinatory range.

paragraphs.text:
The sequence ran through five offerings, known as yi, ji, zai, xie, and yong.
Once the cycle for one ancestor ended, it began again.
A given ancestor seems to have received only one of these rites in any single week.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The cycle is visible only because the late Shang court recorded it so frequently.

timeline.label: Period Two divination
timeline.caption: Early in the documented cycle, the sequence is already regular enough to reconstruct.

timeline.label: Period Five divination
timeline.caption: By the closing phase, the same ordered rotation appears in even more systematic form.

paragraphs.text:
The royal consorts joined this order in a shorter cycle of four rites.
They were honored on the stem day of their own temple names, but their sequence lagged one week behind that of the kings.
The pattern shows how carefully the dead were arranged in ritual time.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Royal consorts were folded into the same ritual ordering that governed the kings.

timeline.label: Period Two divination
timeline.caption: The cycle is already visible in the better-lit middle of the late Shang record.

timeline.label: Period Five divination
timeline.caption: The later record shows how exact and routinized this temporal ordering became.

paragraphs.text:
By Period Five, the system had become remarkably exact.
A single oracle bone might be reserved for one rite alone, even when several ancestors were named on it.
The postfaces could even identify a week by naming the rite that had just been performed.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The ancestral schedule becomes increasingly exact within the late Shang record.

timeline.label: Period Five divination
timeline.caption: By Period Five, ritual assignment had become sharply bone-specific and highly regularized.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou inherited ancestral ordering, though not this exact five-ritual late Shang system.

paragraphs.text:
After the reign of Wu Ding, the inscriptions no longer dwell on Di as the power who orders rain or thunder.
Di does not disappear entirely, but he recedes.
That retreat may reflect growing confidence in the ancestors, indifference to Di, or a sense that his will was too remote to interrogate fruitfully.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: Under Wu Ding, Di still appears more actively in the surviving record.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: After Wu Ding, Di recedes and the record shifts toward a different religious balance.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order inherited Shang sacred concerns but under a changed theological horizon.

paragraphs.text:
At the same time, the ancestors seem to rise in prestige.
Main-line kings could now receive temple names that included the title Di.
The increasingly one-sided divination charges and the king's monopoly over interpretation fit the same movement toward stronger royal assurance.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: Earlier late Shang divination still shows a broader and less one-sided field of interrogation.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: The closing phase strengthens the ancestors and the king's monopoly of interpretation.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: After the conquest, this exact late royal assurance disappeared with the dynasty itself.

paragraphs.text:
For that reason, the old claim that the Shang king was simply a shaman must be treated carefully.
The court certainly inherited older traditions of sacred mediation, and Shang bronzes preserve a world rich in animal and ritual imagery.
But the divination records themselves show a disciplined religious order, not the irregular ecstasy of trance.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Older traditions of sacred mediation lay behind the late court and its ritual imagery.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The inscriptions themselves show a disciplined and formalized religious order, not a frenzy of trance.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later memories of sacred rulership continued, but the late Shang evidence must be read on its own terms.

paragraphs.text:
The late Shang king was a sacred mediator, but a highly formalized one.
His authority rested on regular access to the ancestors, not on inspired disorder.
By this stage, spontaneity would have threatened royal control rather than confirming it.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang king remains a mediator, but in a highly formalized court setting.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: By the final kings, spontaneity would have weakened rather than confirmed royal control.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order preserved sacred rulership while altering the institutions through which it worked.

sections.title: Burial and the making of ancestors

paragraphs.text:
The treatment of the dead was one of the central enterprises of Shang society.
Life was shorter than in the modern world, and death was correspondingly more familiar.
In and around Xiaotun, the excavated skeletons suggest an average age at death of only about thirty-four and a half years.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the late Shang cemeteries, death in Shang society is much harder to grasp at a human scale.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The Xiaotun burials make the late Shang world of frequent death unusually visible.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited many Shang mortuary habits, but not the dynasty's own ancestral center.

paragraphs.text:
Burial did more than dispose of the body.
It helped transform the deceased into an effective ancestor or dependent spirit.
By the late Shang, differences of wealth and rank among the living were projected into death with striking force.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Neolithic mortuary differentiation
timeline.display_date: late Neolithic to early Bronze Age
timeline.caption: Before the late Shang, burial was already becoming a medium for expressing rank and difference among the living.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: By the late Shang, burial had become a major way of turning rank among the living into lasting order among the dead.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou inherited this political use of burial rather than flattening it away.

paragraphs.text:
The royal tombs at Xibeigang, across the Huan River from Xiaotun, marked that principle at its highest level.
Their separation from the main cult center and the scale of their grave goods signaled the special status of kings even after death.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The great royal cemetery belongs to the late Shang court at Anyang.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age follows the royal burials as the next political horizon after Shang.

timeline.label: Xibeigang royal cemetery
timeline.caption: The segregated royal cemetery across the Huan River made Shang kingship visible even after death.

paragraphs.text:
Orientation mattered too.
The great royal tombs were aligned to the cardinal directions, and many other elite burials followed the same logic.
Bodies of high-status dead were usually laid out extended and supine, while sacrificial victims were treated very differently.
Even in death, posture and placement expressed hierarchy.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Neolithic mortuary differentiation
timeline.display_date: late Neolithic to early Bronze Age
timeline.caption: Spatial order in burial had deep prehistoric roots before the late Shang elaborated it further.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Cardinal orientation and bodily treatment made hierarchy visible in late Shang burials.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later mortuary practice retained ordered placement, even after the Shang royal cemetery was abandoned.

sections.title: Grave goods and following in death

paragraphs.text:
The tomb of Lady Hao gives the clearest sense of late Shang opulence.
Though modest beside the looted royal graves, it still held hundreds of bronzes, hundreds of jades, and thousands of cowrie shells.
It was less a grave than an underground treasury of ritual, military, and symbolic wealth.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The tomb belongs to the late Shang world at Anyang.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: Lady Hao was buried under Wu Ding, in the reign that gives the clearest picture of court wealth.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited the broader logic of elite burial, but not this exact Shang court setting.

timeline.label: Lady Hao's tomb
timeline.caption: Her burial makes royal wealth, ritual authority, and the political role of a Shang consort unusually clear.

paragraphs.text:
Smaller elites also received provision for the next world.
At Yinxu West, many burials contained pottery, bronze vessels, weapons, or craft tools.
Ceramic substitutes often stood in for richer bronze furnishings.
What one had done in life was often provided for again in death.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Smaller elite burials across late Shang Anyang preserved the same mortuary logic at lower scale.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age continued to grade burial goods by rank and means.

timeline.label: Yinxu West cemetery
timeline.caption: This cemetery shows how lineage, rank, and burial practice extended well below the highest royal level.

paragraphs.text:
The logic of following in death was even more dramatic.
Some attendants were buried whole near the grave lord, with their own coffins and goods.
Others seem to have served as guards in the waist pit or at the corners of the chamber.
Figure 4.9 makes this graded companionship visible.

figures.label: Figure 4.9
figures.caption: The tomb model makes visible how attendants and dependents were arranged in graded relation to the dead lord, extending hierarchy into death.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The logic of following in death belongs to the late Shang royal mortuary world.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The conquest ended this royal system, though ideas of posthumous service remained powerful.

timeline.label: Xibeigang royal cemetery
timeline.caption: The royal cemetery provides the clearest evidence for graded mortuary accompaniment and dependent sacrifice.

paragraphs.text:
A much harsher level of sacrifice appears in the great royal tombs.
Rows of young male victims, often decapitated or dismembered, were buried in ramps or sacrificial pits.
Figure 4.8 shows one such deposit with grim clarity.
These victims were probably war captives, many of them described in the inscriptions as Qiang.

figures.label: Figure 4.8
figures.caption: The prone, decapitated victims in the ramp show the harsher edge of Shang burial sacrifice and the difference between attendants and captives.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Large-scale human sacrifice in tomb ramps belongs to the late Shang royal mortuary order.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited Shang ritual forms selectively but not this same scale of royal tomb sacrifice.

timeline.label: Xibeigang royal cemetery
timeline.caption: The royal cemetery preserves the clearest archaeological evidence for rows of captives killed for burial.

paragraphs.text:
Late Shang burial formed a full hierarchy.
At the top stood the great ramped royal tombs.
Below them came followers buried with their lords, medium-size elite graves, shallow pits for lesser people, and finally the most meager burials in refuse pits or disused wells.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Neolithic mortuary differentiation
timeline.display_date: late Neolithic to early Bronze Age
timeline.caption: Burial hierarchy did not begin with the Shang, though the late Shang made it especially sharp and visible.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: By the late Shang, the dead were arranged in a full descending order from royal tombs to the poorest burials.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited status-marking burial rather than erasing it.

paragraphs.text:
Architecture itself marked these distinctions.
The largest graves had massive timber chambers with cruciform plans and long ramps.
The four-ramp tombs belonged to kings, while tombs with two ramps or one were for lesser elites.
Even these lesser structures demanded heavy labor and substantial organization.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Neolithic to early Bronze Age
timeline.display_date: late Neolithic to early Bronze Age
timeline.caption: Monumental labor and differentiated burial architecture had deeper roots before the late Shang.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, ramp number, chamber form, and scale made rank visible in architecture itself.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later elite burial would continue to mark rank architecturally, though in different forms.

paragraphs.text:
Below that level, many ordinary burials had only coffins, slight coverings, or no durable protection at all.
The contrast is telling.
Shang mortuary practice did not flatten society before death.
It preserved and displayed rank with relentless clarity.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Ordinary burials in the late Shang lay far below the heavily timbered tombs of the elite.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order continued to bury people unequally rather than leveling status before death.

timeline.label: Yinxu West cemetery
timeline.caption: This cemetery gives the clearest picture of modest late Shang burials with little protection or furnishing.

paragraphs.text:
The care of the dead was therefore also a political economy.
Huge quantities of labor, bronze, jade, animals, food, drink, and human life were poured into tombs and ancestral cult.
The survivors demonstrated their own standing by sustaining the dead in this way.
The demand for such goods must also have driven further production.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Neolithic mortuary differentiation
timeline.display_date: late Neolithic to early Bronze Age
timeline.caption: Long before the late Shang, burial already drew on wealth, labor, and symbolic display.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: By the late Shang, the care of the dead had become a major political economy of production and expenditure.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited the logic that sustaining the dead also displayed the standing of the living.

sections.title: Lineages, local powers, and the Shang world

paragraphs.text:
Many Shang communities seem to have centered on a sacred place, a local power, and a lineage leader who shared the same name.
Place, cult, and political identity were tightly fused.
A community could thus appear as territory, descent group, and ritual body all at once.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier Shang and pre-Shang communities had already tied settlement and sacred power together in local ways.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, many communities still fused place, lineage, cult, and leadership.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later Chinese polities would preserve local ritual-political nodes even within broader state forms.

paragraphs.text:
The Shang also imagined their world in ordered spatial terms.
At the center stood Zhong Shang, the central Shang domain.
Around it lay the Four Lands or Four Regions, arranged by direction.
The word fang could mean side, border, region, or enemy country, which shows how political geography and frontier tension overlapped.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The directional ordering of the world had deeper roots before the best-documented late court.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang inscriptions make Zhong Shang and the Four Lands visible as a structured political cosmos.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Successor states inherited directional political thinking while recasting its institutions.

paragraphs.text:
The bronze insignia and the oracle bones together suggest that the king presided over a confederation of patrilineal groups.
At Yinxu West, even the cemetery can be divided into sectors marked by distinctive assemblages and lineage signs.
The dead preserved the social pattern of the living.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang court appears less as a smooth territory than as a confederation of patrilineal groups.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order inherited lineage politics on a large scale.

timeline.label: Yinxu West cemetery
timeline.caption: The cemetery sectors help show how descent grouping remained visible even among the dead.

paragraphs.text:
That hierarchy extended beyond the royal lineage itself.
Many diviners belonged to lineages that also appear on bronzes, which shows how marriage, service, and ritual skill tied outside groups to the court.
Beyond the core area stood local officers, chiefs, and rulers who led their own dependents in the king's service.
The more distant Hou and Bo could act as allies, tributaries, or uncertain partners on a shifting frontier.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the late archive, outside lineages and local powers are harder to place within the Shang order.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang frontier world was held together by ritual service, shifting alliances, and uneven dependence.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou conquest grew out of this same unstable border world of allies, dependents, and rival lords.

paragraphs.text:
At the core of this order stood the royal lineage itself.
The king's sons, once grown, could become princes who headed their own minor lineages, accompanied the king on hunts and journeys, and took part in ancestral rites.
Prince Hua is one example of such a figure, with his own men, place, obligations, and reports to the court.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The princely order belongs to the late Shang royal lineage at Anyang.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: Prince Hua is visible within the reign that best reveals the political life of royal sons.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later dynasties would continue to use sons and collateral princes as political extensions of the throne.

paragraphs.text:
Not all princes mattered equally.
Only some appear often in the inscriptions, and the king could command princes in a way he could not command outside lords.
Beyond them stood the Many Princes, the broader body of royal cousins and collateral branches who still owed service in war and ritual.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Collateral branches existed before the best-documented late archive, though their role is less clearly seen.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, only some princes remained close enough to the king to matter heavily in the record.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Successor polities would likewise struggle to order the service of collateral kin around the throne.

paragraphs.text:
Other magnates, though not called princes, also led dependents and cast their insignia into ritual bronzes.
They seem to have been more distant offshoots of the royal house, still tied to older ancestors but no longer close enough to count among the king's sons.
Some may have formed defensive nodes on the western approaches to the Shang heartland.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: More distant offshoots of the royal house grew over time beyond the inner princely circle.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, such magnates could still lead dependents while standing farther from the main line.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The weakening of outer allies and collateral nodes helped prepare the later fall of the dynasty.

paragraphs.text:
The Shang left no simple king list carved as a genealogy.
Scholars must reconstruct the royal sequence from the schedule of sacrifices, and even that sequence reflects a ritual view of the past.
Some collateral temple names may preserve traces of incorporated lineages or regions.
Succession itself remains partly uncertain, and later tradition remembered periods of rivalry among brothers and nephews.
The stem patterns in royal temple names are striking, but whether they reflect alternating descent groups or ritual calendrical logic remains unsettled.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: Succession and temple naming belong to the whole dynastic problem, not only to the final kings.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The sacrificial schedule of the late court is the main basis from which scholars reconstruct the royal sequence.

timeline.label: Han retrospective reconstruction
timeline.caption: Much remembered rivalry among brothers and nephews comes through much later textual reconstruction rather than direct late Shang narration.

sections.title: Royal women and a narrower heartland

paragraphs.text:
Royal women helped bind the dynasty to other communities.
Their names often preserved signs of their social origins, and some took part in preparing the scapulas and plastrons used in divination.
Their ability to bear sons was therefore not merely domestic.
It shaped the future of lineage, alliance, and succession.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Royal marriage ties must already have mattered before the late archive becomes rich enough to show them clearly.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang record, royal women appear as alliance-bearers, ritual participants, and mothers of future heirs.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later dynasties would continue to bind marriage, fertility, and political structure together.

paragraphs.text:
Lady Hao shows how powerful such a woman could become.
Buried with extraordinary splendor, she later received major cult as Mother Xin.
Her son, the prince later remembered as Xiao Ji, seems to have stood at one point as heir apparent.
Marriage, maternity, and political structure were inseparable.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Lady Hao belongs to the late Shang world of royal cult and lineage politics.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: Her prominence belongs to the reign that most vividly documents court life and succession.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited the political importance of royal women even as the Shang court itself vanished.

timeline.label: Lady Hao's tomb
timeline.caption: Her burial and later cult make her the clearest single example of an eminent Shang royal woman.

paragraphs.text:
Map 4.1 helps situate the heartland of this order.
The royal center at Xiaotun was surrounded by allied settlements, workshops, cemeteries, and lineage sites spread through a core zone near the Taihang foothills.
This was the densest and most secure region of Shang power.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the final Anyang center, the dynasty's political geography is broader in outline but less securely mapped.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang heartland clustered around Xiaotun in a tight core of ritual, productive, and lineage sites.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The conquest ended this Shang core as a ruling center, even though many of its habits survived.

paragraphs.text:
Shang cultural sites appear across a very wide area, from the north China plain into many surrounding provinces.
But culturally Shang was not the same as politically Shang.
The late dynasty may well have held a narrower and more defensive heartland than older tradition imagined.
Its frontiers remained porous, and the area in which the king could move with confidence was probably not vast.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Shang cultural expansion reached widely before and beyond the final Anyang core.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: By the late dynasty, political control was probably narrower than the spread of Shang-style material culture.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou inherited a world of porous frontiers and uneven control rather than a neat, uniform territorial block.

paragraphs.text:
The Anyang region in the twelfth and eleventh centuries before Christ seems to have been wetter and warmer than it is now.
Rain was probably more evenly distributed through the year, which would have favored spring planting.
