figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier climatic conditions matter, but the best direct evidence comes only from the late Shang world.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The twelfth and eleventh century climate around Anyang seems to have favored cultivation more than the modern regime does.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited the same north China ecological field after the fall of Shang.

paragraphs.text:
The Shang world was still, above all, an agricultural world.
Its communities lived in small settlements surrounded by fields, and the king's divinations about harvest often concerned not just his own crops but the wider lands of Shang.
His ritual responsibility for food production reached across the Four Lands.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier Shang agriculture is less directly visible before the late archive.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang record, the king's ritual concern extends from his own fields to the wider lands of Shang.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou would continue to link kingship, agriculture, and ritual responsibility in their own way.

paragraphs.text:
The king also controlled important royal farms.
These were cleared and worked by labor gangs under officers, and they helped support the ruling house, privileged elites, craft industries, and the army.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier royal agriculture must mostly be inferred before the late Shang archive becomes dense.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang court controlled royal farms that sustained households, workshops, and armies.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited the idea that the ruler's material base depended on organized cultivation.

paragraphs.text:
The inscriptions show how closely the king watched rain, planting, inspections, and harvest.
He might decide whether to inspect the millet, command laborers into the fields, or ask whether a certain officer's crops would prosper in a named place.
Agriculture was not beneath kingship.
It was one of the chief arenas in which kingship acted.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The close ritual supervision of agriculture belongs to the late Shang court.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: The richest evidence for royal decisions about planting, inspection, and harvest comes especially from Wu Ding's reign.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later dynasties would preserve the ruler's symbolic responsibility for agriculture in altered institutional form.

paragraphs.text:
Archaeology supports this picture of organized labor.
Large numbers of stone sickles have been found around Xiaotun, including caches so large that they suggest planned production and storage for the harvest.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The find belongs to the late Shang working landscape around Xiaotun.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited the need for coordinated harvest labor even after the Shang court fell.

timeline.label: Xiaotun sickle caches
timeline.caption: Large stored groups of stone sickles reveal organized production and coordinated labor for harvest.

paragraphs.text:
Millet was the staple crop of the Shang.
The diviners spoke of millet and standing grain in broad terms, which suggests that their concern was the harvest as a whole rather than fine botanical distinctions among varieties.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The wider history of millet cultivation long precedes the late Shang archive.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang record, the harvest matters more than exact crop taxonomy.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Millet would remain central in the agrarian world that followed Shang.

paragraphs.text:
When crops failed or suffered blight, the Shang did not experience that merely as an agricultural problem.
They asked which Power was harming the grain.
The Mountain, the River, or Di himself might be named.
A good harvest therefore brought not only food, but a sense of spiritual vindication.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier sacred readings of crop failure lie behind the late archive but are less directly visible.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang world, blight could be interpreted as harm inflicted by powers such as the Mountain, the River, or Di.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later Chinese agriculture remained ritually charged, though not in the same late Shang divinatory form.

paragraphs.text:
The opening of new land was one of the king's own affairs.
The inscriptions suggest drainage and field making, but not great irrigation works.
Officers and labor gangs were ordered to clear land, raise field boundaries, and bring new ground under cultivation.
Hunting and agriculture overlapped in this process, since burning brush for the hunt also helped clear and fertilize future fields.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the best-documented late phase, land opening and expansion remain much harder to see directly.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, clearing land, hunting, and extending cultivation belong to the king's own affairs.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited this linkage between territorial reach, labor, and cultivated land.

paragraphs.text:
The same court that managed fields also managed herds.
Bone workshops drew on cattle, sheep, pigs, deer, dogs, and horses.
Titles such as the Many Dog officers and the Many Horse officers point to organized animal management.
The scale of sacrifice, with offerings of cattle, sheep, pigs, and dogs in large numbers, makes clear that livestock husbandry was substantial.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier Shang husbandry must be inferred more indirectly before the late archive and workshops speak so clearly.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang court managed herds on a scale sufficient for both workshop supply and major sacrifice.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Livestock management remained tied to elite provisioning and ritual after the fall of Shang.

paragraphs.text:
Part of Shang power rested on what allies and dependents brought to the king.
They supplied people, especially captives, for sacrifice, and they also placed labor contingents at his disposal.
Some of this support had real economic weight, but much of it also carried political and ritual meaning.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Dependence and tribute did not begin with the late Shang, though the archive then records them more clearly.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, people, captives, and labor contingents helped sustain royal sacrifice and power.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order inherited political dependence expressed through tribute and service.

paragraphs.text:
Animals too could arrive as tribute.
A dependent might bring white horses or cattle.
Yet such offerings seem to have mattered less for feeding the state than for displaying loyalty and confirming the king's central position.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Prestige gifts and dependent offerings belong to a broader Bronze Age political world before and beyond the late Shang.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang record, animals given to the king matter as signs of loyalty as much as as raw economic supply.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later Chinese states continued to encode dependence through symbolic gifts as well as through material support.

paragraphs.text:
The most important tribute items in the inscriptions were the turtle shells and cattle scapulas used for divination.
These could arrive in very large shipments, and the court recorded their origins, numbers, preparation, and delivery with care.
The exchange was revealing.
Others sent the raw material through which the king addressed the powers, and the king in turn bestowed gifts such as cowries, cattle, sheep, or weapons.
This was not yet a rigid tribute system.
It was a network of dependence expressed through symbolic exchange.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: No earlier Shang evidence reveals the logistical basis of divination so concretely.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, shells and scapulas moved into the royal center as both ritual supplies and political offerings.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The conquest ended this exact exchange network, though writing and divination continued in new forms.

paragraphs.text:
The Xiaotun heartland contained a wide range of specialized workshops.
Bronze foundries, bone workshops, and other production areas show a scale of organization more extensive than what had existed in the Erligang age.
Different sites concentrated on different outputs, such as ritual vessels, tools and weapons, or bone ornaments.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier Shang metallurgy and craft production laid the background for the denser late workshop system.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang heartland concentrated specialized production in multiple coordinated workshop zones.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age inherited large-scale craft organization even as its centers and outputs changed.

paragraphs.text:
Labor conscripts were one of the deepest foundations of Shang power.
They built temple palaces and tombs, farmed the land, hauled supplies, and made the objects that sustained elite life and ritual.
In this sense the late Shang state stood on organized human effort no less than on bronze or divination.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Neolithic to early Bronze Age
timeline.display_date: late Neolithic to early Bronze Age
timeline.caption: Long before the late Shang, large communal projects already depended on organized labor.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: By the late Shang, conscripted labor had become one of the deepest foundations of state power.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later Chinese states would continue to rest heavily on mobilized human effort.

paragraphs.text:
It is better to speak here of graded dependency than of simple slavery or freedom.
The inscriptions refer often to the zhong and the ren.
The zhong seem to have been a smaller body of permanently attached dependents, often tied to a lord or lineage.
The ren were a broader pool of men who could be mobilized in far larger numbers.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The full social history behind these categories stretches earlier than the surviving late archive.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang inscriptions, zhong and ren mark different degrees of dependence within the laboring order.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later social orders inherited dependence and gradation without preserving these categories unchanged.

paragraphs.text:
The two groups could overlap in function.
Both appear in agriculture and warfare.
But the zhong were counted in smaller numbers and seem less expendable, while the ren could be raised in the thousands.
The contrast suggests different degrees of attachment, privilege, and replaceability within the same general order of service.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: The same overlap of war, agriculture, and service likely has deeper roots before the late archive speaks clearly.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, zhong and ren could overlap in function while remaining unequal in scale and replaceability.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order inherited a world in which the same manpower could serve both field and battlefield.

paragraphs.text:
The royal workshops produced a substantial range of weapons, from dagger axes and spearheads to arrowheads, helmets, shields, and chariot fittings.
Under Wu Ding the Shang fought major campaigns against powers such as the Tufang, Gongfang, and Qiangfang.
The king might lead in person, ally himself with other leaders, or send out major commanders.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The campaigns belong to the late Shang military world centered at Anyang.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: Wu Ding's reign provides the fullest evidence for large-scale Shang warfare and campaigning.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age would inherit many military practices after defeating Shang itself.

paragraphs.text:
The inscriptions often present these campaigns alongside proposed levies of three thousand or five thousand men.
On one occasion the number reaches ten thousand.
The troops seem to have been arranged in left, center, and right divisions, while archers and horse officers were also counted in large, orderly units.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang army could already be counted and mobilized in very large formations.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: The richest surviving evidence for proposed levies comes from the reign of Wu Ding.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The Zhou conquest shows that large-scale mobilization was not a uniquely Shang capacity by the end.

paragraphs.text:
Chariots were important, but they did not make the Shang army into a purely chariot force.
Most soldiers must still have fought on foot.
The evidence also hints at a standing core of military organization beneath the larger ad hoc mobilizations.
The late Shang state was not fully bureaucratic, but it had already learned how to count, group, arm, and direct men on a considerable scale.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Earlier Bronze Age warfare forms the background to the late Shang mixture of foot soldiers and chariots.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang, chariots mattered, but infantry still carried most of the fighting burden.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age would continue to use chariots while developing military organization in new directions.

paragraphs.text:
The king's choice of the right man for the right task was itself a matter for divination.
The inscriptions often ask whether a given officer should pursue an enemy, lead a force, or undertake some royal business.
In that sense personnel decision was still highly personal and sacralized.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: Personnel choice remains a late Shang royal practice visible in the inscriptions.

timeline.label: Reign of Wu Ding
timeline.caption: The reign of Wu Ding preserves especially many divinations about who should undertake major tasks.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: Later states would make appointments more routinized, but the linkage between personnel and legitimacy would remain.

paragraphs.text:
Yet the titles of some groups already suggest something more impersonal in the making.
The Many Horse officers, the Many Dog officers, the Many Artisans, and the Many Archers sound less like household retainers than like recurrent functional bodies.
The same is true of the many Junior Servitors who seem to have held named duties at court.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: Before the late archive, the growth of recurring functional groups is much less directly visible.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: In the late Shang record, titles such as Many Horse or Many Archers suggest more durable bodies than mere household retainers.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age would push further toward more impersonal administrative groupings.

paragraphs.text:
The divination archive itself reveals a habit of careful routine.
The court noted where bones and shells came from, who prepared them, when they were delivered, who divined them, how the charges were phrased, and even how the cracks were counted.
Certain bones were reserved for certain rituals and could be reused after intervals of nonuse.
What survives is not just religion.
It is ordered record keeping.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Earlier Shang before Anyang
timeline.caption: No earlier Shang evidence preserves administrative routine at this level of detail.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The late Shang archive itself records a disciplined practice of storage, reuse, delivery, dating, and crack counting.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor order inherited and extended the value of written routine in political life.

paragraphs.text:
Other inscriptions point in the same direction.
The Shang counted prisoners, booty, and animals.
They used tablets, registers, and written pledges in ritual and military settings.
A written script did not create Shang power by itself, but it greatly strengthened the court's ability to organize labor, preserve decisions, and make authority durable.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The script became one of the dynasty's major political inheritances, not merely a late curiosity.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The successor age continued to use writing for rule as well as for ritual.

timeline.label: Eastern Zhou
timeline.caption: By the later Zhou world, registers, records, and written claims had become even more central to political life.

paragraphs.text:
The later record gives the impression of a core area that was relatively secure from direct invasion.
The Xiaotun moat seems to have choked up by the end of the dynasty, and the last inscriptions no longer dwell on enemy raids in the old way.
Whether that reflects reality or simply changed divinatory habits, the late Shang court increasingly appears as a raiding power rather than a constantly raided one.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The larger late Shang field still frames the dynasty's final posture.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: The last kings appear to rule a more secure but also more inward-looking core area.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: This late stability ended with the rise of the Zhou, the next ruling horizon after Shang.

paragraphs.text:
Yet this stability was bought within a smaller political world.
After Wu Ding, western alliances weakened, and former supporters or allies slipped beyond Shang control.
The last kings could still mount major expeditions, such as the long campaign against the Renfang, but they seem to have ruled within a narrower sphere than before.
In the middle of the eleventh century before Christ, one former ally turned enemy, the Zhou, destroyed the dynasty.

figures.label: Map 4.1
figures.caption: The map gathers Xiaotun, workshops, cemeteries, and nearby allied sites into one view of the dense core of late Shang power.

timeline.label: Late Shang at Anyang
timeline.caption: The contraction belongs to the late Shang world as a whole.

timeline.label: Final Shang kings
timeline.caption: By the final kings, campaigns could still be mounted, but within a smaller political sphere.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The next horizon is the Zhou order that emerged from former alliance into conquest.

timeline.label: Zhou conquest of Shang
timeline.caption: The Zhou overthrow in the middle of the eleventh century BC ended the dynasty's independent political life.

paragraphs.text:
What endured was immense.
The Shang left behind a patrimonial sacred kingship centered on ancestors, a heavy investment in bronzes and burial, and a state sustained by mobilized labor.
They also passed on a script used in government as well as ritual, and the sixty day ganzhi calendar.
They also left a durable political habit.
Religion, administration, lineage, and power could be woven together into one order.
In that sense, despite their defeat at Muye, the Shang long outlived their own kingdom.

timeline.label: Shang dynasty
timeline.caption: The dynasty's institutions mattered far beyond the years of its own rule.

timeline.label: Western Zhou successor age
timeline.caption: The first successor age inherited Shang kingship, ritual, writing, and calendrical habits on a large scale.

timeline.label: Eastern Zhou
timeline.caption: Much later Chinese political culture still lived within forms first powerfully assembled under the Shang.

timeline.label: Battle of Muye
timeline.caption: Military defeat ended the kingdom, but not the afterlife of Shang political culture.
